insights: Incident distribution across all five categories is perfectly uniform, with exactly 100 incidents (20% each), indicating no categorical discrepancy or concentration risk.

The uniformity extends beyond categories to all major operational dimensions—priority, location, assignment_group, and assigned_to—each showing exact 100-incident balance, confirming systemic equilibrium in assignment logic.
The absence of imbalance across all examined dimensions suggests the assignment mechanism operates with deterministic fairness and no hidden bias or drift in routing behavior.
With zero variance in category-level incident volume, resource allocation, monitoring focus, or category-specific SLA planning cannot be justified on the basis of distributional disparity.
summary: The analysis conclusively shows no discrepancy or imbalance in incident distribution across categories—or any other key operational dimension—as each category and attribute group contains exactly 100 incidents. This perfect uniformity reflects a highly controlled, bias-free assignment process with no evidence of overloading, underutilization, or structural skew. Consequently, business decisions predicated on category-level imbalances—such as targeted staffing, priority governance, or root-cause triage by category—are not supported by the data. Next focus should shift to evaluating performance metrics (e.g., resolution time, reassignment rate, severity escalation) where meaningful variation may exist.
